Some express themselves through bold colors and patterns, while others prefer neutral tones that quietly blend into the background. Neither is wrong. It is simply expression. It is human nature trying to show the world who we are, or maybe even who we hope to become.

Sometimes clothes are armor.

A suit can hide insecurity. A uniform can carry responsibility. Makeup and fashion can mask pain. We can look perfectly put together while silently carrying heartbreak, anxiety, grief, or loneliness underneath it all. The world often compliments appearances without ever realizing the battle happening inside the person wearing them.

That perspective matters.

Because maybe the person wearing wrinkled clothes is overwhelmed. Maybe the teenager dressing differently is trying to figure out where they belong. Maybe the elderly person wearing the same outfit repeatedly is struggling more than anyone realizes. Maybe the person overly dressed for an occasion simply needed to feel good about themselves for one day.

We judge people quickly by appearance. Humanity has always done it. But faith reminds us that God sees far deeper than fabric and outward presentation.

Scripture reminds us in 1 Samuel 16:7 that “People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.”

That verse humbles me every single time.

We spend so much energy trying to look acceptable to others while God is focused on the condition of our spirit. The kindness in our hearts matters more than the logo on our shirt. The compassion we show matters more than the shoes we wear. Character will always outlast style trends.

Now don’t get me wrong, I still appreciate good clothes. I mean, if I find a shirt that fits right, hides stress-eating evidence, and survives corgi hair, it practically deserves an award. And if you own dogs, especially corgis, you already know your wardrobe eventually becomes less about fashion and more about asking yourself, “Will the fur show on this color?”

Black clothing owners understand this struggle deeply.

But maybe clothing also teaches us another important lesson: we are allowed to change.

Styles evolve. Seasons change. We outgrow certain clothes physically and emotionally. The outfit that represented who you were five years ago may not fit the person you are becoming today. And that is okay. Growth often requires us to step into something new.

The same is true spiritually and emotionally.

Sometimes we carry old labels, old shame, old insecurities, and old versions of ourselves far too long. We wear them daily without realizing they no longer fit who God created us to be. Fear. Rejection. Self-doubt. Hurt. We become comfortable carrying things that were never meant to define us forever.

Maybe it is time to take some of those things off.

Maybe it is time to stop dressing your spirit in criticism and disappointment.

Maybe it is time to wear grace again.

Confidence again.

Hope again.

Love again.

Because what you wear on the inside eventually matters far more than what you wear on the outside.

And the beautiful thing is this: every day gives us another opportunity to choose how we present ourselves to the world, not just through fashion, but through kindness, humility, compassion, and authenticity.

The best dressed people are not always the ones wearing expensive clothes.

Sometimes they are simply the people wearing kindness well.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Rollercoaster We Never Asked to Ride
excerpt: Life can have up and downs.
content:
The Rollercoaster We Never Asked to Ride

I have never been much of a rollercoaster person. Truthfully, the idea of being strapped into a giant metal contraption while someone confidently says, “Enjoy the ride!” has never really brought me peace. I prefer keeping my feet firmly planted on the ground where Jesus and common sense intended them to be.

But even though I avoid rollercoasters at amusement parks, life still seems determined to put all of us on one anyway.

There are unexpected climbs, sudden drops, sharp turns, moments of fear, and seasons where everything feels completely out of control. One minute life feels calm and manageable, and the next minute you are hanging on emotionally wondering how things shifted so fast.

You know the kind of ride life can become. The slow climb upward where you can hear every click of uncertainty beneath you. The anticipation builds. Your stomach tightens. You look around wondering if maybe getting off would have been the smarter choice. But once life starts moving, there is no stopping the momentum completely.

There are seasons where we slowly climb toward goals, dreams, healing, relationships, purpose, or simply trying to find peace within ourselves. Those climbs can feel exhausting. Sometimes we wonder why progress feels so slow. Why the answers are not immediate. Why God feels quiet in the middle of the climb.

But often the climb is preparing us for the view.

The difficult part is that life does not allow us to stay at the top forever. Eventually come the drops. The unexpected diagnosis. The heartbreak. The rejection. The grief. The anxiety. The loneliness. The moments where life feels completely out of control and we are gripping onto faith with trembling hands just trying to hold ourselves together.

And if we are honest, some waves in life hit harder than others.

I think many of us spend so much time trying to avoid the hard moments that we forget the purpose of the ride itself. We pray for smooth roads with no bumps, no fear, no uncertainty, and no pain. But growth rarely happens in the calm stretches of life. Strength is often built in the moments where we feel shaken the most.

Even the ocean teaches us this lesson.

Waves rise.
Waves crash.
Waves retreat.
Then they rise again.

Life moves in seasons the same way. Nobody stays on the mountaintop forever, and thankfully, nobody stays in the valley forever either.

There have been moments in my own life where I felt emotionally upside down. Feeling disconnected. Questioning my worth. Wondering why certain people walked away so easily or why some doors closed without explanation. There are moments where life feels unfair. Moments where you silently ask God, “Are you sure this thing is still on the tracks?”

Yet somehow, we keep moving forward.

Maybe that is what resilience really is. Not pretending life is easy. Not forcing fake smiles. But continuing to hold on through the chaos while trusting that God still sees the full track ahead even when we cannot.

And maybe the people around us are riding waves too.

The cashier who seems frustrated.
The friend who suddenly becomes distant.
The coworker who looks exhausted.
The stranger sitting quietly in their car before walking into work.

We never fully know what kind of emotional ride someone else is surviving.

That is why kindness matters so much.

Sometimes people are not difficult.
Sometimes they are simply tired from the ride.

As I have gotten older, I have realized something important about waves and rollercoasters: they are temporary experiences. The fear passes. The storm settles. The sharp turns eventually straighten out.

And often when we finally catch our breath, we realize we survived moments we once believed would completely break us.

Maybe today you feel stuck in the middle of a steep drop.
Maybe you are climbing toward something uncertain.
Maybe life feels loud, shaky, and completely overwhelming.

But keep holding on.

The ride is not over yet.

There is still purpose in your story.
There is still beauty in your journey.
There is still growth happening in the middle of your uncertainty.

And sometimes the greatest testimony is simply this:

“I didn’t quit.”

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: When Life Starts Looking Like a Road Sign
excerpt: A sign we all need to see and understand.
content:
When Life Starts Looking Like a Road Sign

Most of us don’t think twice about street signs. They’re part of the routine. You drive, you see them, you respond, and you move on. Stop. Yield. No right turn. Merge. Sharp curve. One lane road. Speed limit. Exit. 

They guide us, warn us, and keep us on track.

But what if they’re doing more than that?

What if those same signs are quietly reflecting the way we’re supposed to move through life?

What if they’re not just directions for the road… but reminders for the soul?

The stop sign is usually the one we try to rush through. Even on the road, people barely come to a full stop anymore. And in life, it’s no different. We keep going, keep pushing, keep filling our schedules and our minds until there’s no space left to breathe. But God calls us to something different. “Be still, and know that I am God.” There are moments when stopping isn’t optional… it’s necessary. Necessary to hear Him. 

Necessary to reset. Necessary to avoid running straight into something we were never meant to face at full speed. Stopping isn’t falling behind. It’s realigning.

Then comes the yield sign, and this is where things get personal. Yielding means stepping back. Letting someone else go first. Trusting timing that isn’t your own. And that goes against everything in us. We want control. We want answers. We want movement. But faith often requires waiting. It requires trusting that God sees the full picture when we only see what’s in front of us. Yielding is not losing your place… it’s trusting that your place is being prepared.

The no right turn sign doesn’t get talked about much, but it might be one of the clearest messages. It’s a boundary. A firm “not this way.” And if we’re honest, those are the signs we struggle with the most. We see them in life. We feel them. We even know deep down that a certain direction isn’t right for us… but we try anyway. We convince ourselves we can make it work. But sometimes God puts up a clear sign that says, “Not this path.” Not to punish you… but to protect you. Not every open road is meant for you to take.
The merge sign speaks to transitions, and transitions are rarely comfortable. Merging requires awareness, patience, and timing.

 You can’t force your way in without causing disruption. Life is full of these moments… new jobs, new environments, new relationships, new seasons. You don’t always feel ready, and you don’t always know where you fit. But God doesn’t call you to force your place. He calls you to trust the process. To move with intention. To find your place in what He is already setting into motion.

The sharp curve ahead is where life can feel overwhelming. These are the moments that catch you off guard. The sudden change. The unexpected loss. The situation you never planned for. And your instinct is to keep going the same speed, hoping things will straighten out. But curves don’t work that way. They force you to slow down. To focus. To hold steady. And in those moments, faith reminds us that even when the road turns sharply, God is still guiding every step. You may not see what’s around the bend… but He does.

The one lane road represents those seasons where life narrows. Where distractions are stripped away. Where options feel limited. And at first, that can feel frustrating. Confining, even. But sometimes, less is exactly what we need. Less noise. Less confusion. Less pulling us in every direction. Just one path forward.

 Clear. Focused. Intentional. Jesus even spoke of the narrow road… not because it’s easy, but because it leads somewhere meaningful. 

Those seasons refine you. 

They teach you discipline. They strengthen your faith.

The speed limit is one we all struggle with. We want things faster. We want answers now. We want growth without waiting, healing without process, success without time. But God’s timing doesn’t operate on our urgency. It operates on purpose. 

There’s a pace designed for your life, and when you try to outrun it, you don’t just miss moments… you risk breaking something along the way. Slowing down isn’t a setback. It’s alignment. It’s trusting that what’s meant for you will come at the right time, not just the fastest time.

And then there’s the exit sign.

This is where faith really gets tested.

Exits require movement. They require leaving something behind. Sometimes something comfortable. Sometimes something familiar. Sometimes something you thought would last longer than it did. And we resist it. 

We stay too long. We hold on too tightly. But sometimes, the exit is exactly where God is leading you. Not away from something good… but toward something better. Toward growth. Toward purpose. 

Toward a path you couldn’t see until you were willing to let go of the one you were on.

When you begin to see these signs through a different lens, they stop being ordinary.

They become meaningful.

They become reminders that God is always guiding, always speaking, always present… even in the simplest parts of your day.

So the next time you’re driving and you see one of those signs, don’t just glance at it and move on.

Let it sit with you for a moment.

Ask yourself what it might be saying beyond the surface.

Because sometimes, the clearest messages in life aren’t the loudest ones.

They’re the quiet reminders placed right in front of you… over and over again… until you finally see them for what they are.

Guidance.
Direction.
Grace.

And a reminder that you are never navigating this road alone.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Using Our Time, Treasures, and Talent to Inspire Others
excerpt: What are you doing with your time, your talent, and your treasures?
content:
Using Our Time, Treasures, and Talent to Inspire Others
We’ve all been given something in this life.

Not the same things. Not in the same amounts. But enough.

Enough time.

Enough treasure.

Enough talent.

And the question isn’t whether we have them… it’s what we’re doing with them.

Time is the easiest to waste and the hardest to replace. It slips through our hands quietly, in busy days and distracted moments. But when we slow down long enough to really see it, we realize something important… the time we give to others is never wasted. A conversation, a visit, a few extra minutes to listen or encourage… those are the moments people carry with them. That’s where connection is built. That’s where impact begins.

Treasure isn’t just about money. It’s about what you’ve been blessed with. It’s your ability to give, to help, to meet a need. Sometimes that looks like something big. Most of the time, it doesn’t. It’s the quiet acts. The thoughtful gestures. The willingness to say, “I have something… and I’m going to use it to help someone else.” That kind of generosity has a ripple effect far beyond what we see.

And then there’s talent.

Every single person has something they bring to the table. Something that can encourage, teach, lead, or simply make someone’s day a little better. Too often, we downplay it. 

We think it’s not enough or not important. But what comes naturally to you might be exactly what someone else needs. When you use your gifts with intention, you create space for others to grow, to feel seen, to believe they can do the same.

Here’s the part that matters most…

You don’t have to have it all figured out to make a difference.
You don’t have to wait until you have more time, more money, or more confidence.
You start where you are.
You give what you can.
You use what you’ve been given.

Because when time, treasure, and talent come together with purpose… they don’t just change your life.
They inspire someone else’s.

And the truth is, you may never fully see the impact you’ve made. You may never hear the full story of how your kindness, your presence, or your effort helped someone through a moment they were struggling with.

But that doesn’t mean it didn’t matter.

It means it mattered more than you know.

So today, maybe the perspective is simple…
Be present with your time.
Be generous with your treasure.
Be intentional with your talent.
Because a life well-lived isn’t measured by what we kept…
It’s measured by what we gave…
and who we inspired along the way.
-Dr. Nick

title: BINGO Chaos
excerpt: B-I-N-G-O.
content:
Earlier this week, I took my son to play BINGO for the first time. Now, I did my best to prepare him. I gave him the rundown… the pace, the passion, the personalities. I told him, “This is not just a quiet little game.”
And let me tell you, I wasn’t wrong.

First off, let’s clear something up. BINGO is not just an “old person game.” Not even close. The room was filled with people of all ages. Young adults, families, seasoned pros who clearly treat this like a competitive sport. It was a full house of energy, anticipation, and just the right amount of chaos.

And the rules? Oh, they matter.

If the caller goes too slow, people get restless. Too fast? You’ll hear about it. Loudly. If a number is called that doesn’t belong, like slipping an “N” into a B/O-only game, the room reacts like a referee just blew a bad call in the final seconds of a championship game.

There are no quiet corrections. Only passionate ones.

Then there are the setups. Everyone has their system. Their lucky trinkets. Multiple daubers lined up like tools on a workbench. Cards spread out with precision. It’s not just playing… it’s a process. A ritual.
And the chatter.

If you’ve never sat and just listened to a BINGO hall, you’re missing out on one of life’s great shows. Conversations about grandkids, work, life struggles, dinner plans, and everything in between. Some stories are hilarious. Some are heartfelt. Some are just plain random. But all of them matter in that moment.
Sitting there, watching it all unfold, I found myself thinking…

What is it about something this simple that brings people together?

Because at its core, BINGO is just numbers on a card. That’s it. No complex rules. No fancy technology required. Just numbers being called and people paying attention.

And yet, it creates something bigger.

It creates connection.

It creates a space where people show up, sit side by side, and share a moment. They laugh. They complain.

 They celebrate a win like it’s the biggest victory of the week. And for some, maybe it is.

In a world that feels increasingly complicated, where everything moves fast and conversations are often reduced to quick messages and notifications, BINGO feels… refreshingly simple.
It reminds us that we don’t need much to feel connected.

We don’t need perfect conditions or elaborate plans. Sometimes all it takes is a shared experience, a little structure, and a willingness to be present.

There’s something beautiful about that.

It made me think about how often we overlook the simple things in life. The moments that don’t seem big enough to matter. The everyday interactions that quietly shape who we are and how we connect with others.

We chase big milestones, big moments, big successes. And those are important. But somewhere along the way, we forget that meaning is often found in the ordinary.

In a room full of people, a stack of cards, and a voice calling out numbers.

BINGO, of all things, reminded me that life doesn’t always need to be complicated to be meaningful.
Sometimes, it’s about showing up. Paying attention. Sharing a laugh. Getting a little competitive. Maybe even getting a little loud when the caller messes up.

And walking away with more than you came in with, even if you didn’t win a single game.

Because in the end, it was never just about the numbers.

It was about the people.

And maybe that’s the real win.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Finding JOY—The Order That Changes Everything
excerpt: Do you know JOY?
content:
Finding JOY—The Order That Changes Everything

Think about the word joy for a moment. Not the kind that comes and goes with a good day or a lucky break, but the kind that sticks with you even when life doesn’t go according to plan. 

We all want it. We chase it in different ways, hoping the next achievement, the next moment, or even the next quiet day will finally bring it. But what if joy isn’t something you stumble into?

 What if it’s something you build—intentionally, steadily, and with purpose?

There’s a simple way to look at it: J-O-Y. Not just a word, but a way of living that, when you get it right, seems to bring everything else back into focus.

J is for Jesus.

Now stay with me here. This isn’t about checking a box on Sunday morning or posting a verse and calling it a day. This is about foundation. When your life is rooted in something bigger than yourself, something steady and unchanging, it shifts how you see everything else. Storms still come. Hard days still show up uninvited. But there’s a peace underneath it all that doesn’t move. That’s where real joy starts. Not in perfection, but in presence. Not in control, but in trust.

O is for Others.

And this is where things start to get real. Because putting others before yourself goes against just about everything the world pushes these days. We’re told to look out for number one, protect our time, guard our energy. And sure, there’s a place for boundaries. But there’s also something deeply fulfilling about stepping outside of yourself and showing up for someone else. A kind word. A helping hand. Listening when it would be easier to scroll. It doesn’t have to be grand. Most of the time, it’s the small, unnoticed moments that matter most. Funny thing is, when you start pouring into others, you realize your own cup doesn’t run dry like you thought it would.

Y is for Yourself.

Now this part tends to get misunderstood. Putting yourself last doesn’t mean ignoring yourself. It doesn’t mean running on empty or pretending you’re fine when you’re not. It means trusting that when your priorities are in the right order, you’ll still be taken care of. It means giving yourself grace, taking time to breathe, and recognizing that you’re human. You matter too. But you don’t have to be at the center of everything to find peace.

And that’s really what this all comes down to. Order.

We spend so much of our lives trying to rearrange things. Putting ourselves first, chasing happiness, trying to control outcomes. And somehow, we end up feeling more off-balance than ever. But when you flip it, when you go back to something simple like J-O-Y, things start to settle.

Jesus. Others. Yourself.

Not because it’s easy. Not because it’s trendy. But because it works.

Joy isn’t loud. It doesn’t demand attention. It shows up in quiet moments. In conversations that matter. In showing up for people. In trusting God when you don’t have all the answers.

And maybe that’s the lesson here. Joy was never meant to be complicated. We just forgot how to keep it simple.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others

title: The Stone or the Brick—Which Are You?
excerpt: Are you a brick or a stone?
content:
Last night at church, something simple stuck with me. You know how that happens sometimes. It’s not always the long explanations or the big moments. Sometimes it’s one image, one comparison, that quietly sits with you and won’t let go.

The focus was on the stone at the tomb of Jesus. That stone wasn’t meant to move. It was placed there to seal something shut. To mark an ending. To say, this is over.

But we all know how that story goes.

That stone didn’t stay put.

And then came a comparison I hadn’t really thought about before. A brick versus a stone.

A brick is structured. Manufactured. Designed to stay exactly where it’s placed. Stack it, line it up, build something with it, and it does its job. It holds firm. It doesn’t shift. It doesn’t adapt. It doesn’t move unless something forces it to.

There’s a certain comfort in that, isn’t there? Predictable. Stable. Safe.

But also… stagnant.

A stone, on the other hand, is different. It’s not perfectly shaped. It’s been through things. Weathered. Worn down. Moved by water, by time, by pressure. And because of that, it changes. It ends up in places it wasn’t originally meant to be. It gets turned over. It exposes what’s underneath. It contributes to something bigger simply by being willing to move.

That got me thinking.

Which one are we?

Because if we’re honest, it’s easy to become a brick. We find our spot in life, we get comfortable, we build our routines, and we stay there. Same mindset. Same patterns. Same way of doing things. We convince ourselves that staying put is the safest way to live.

And maybe it is.

But nothing grows there.

Being a moving stone? That’s different. That takes a willingness to be uncomfortable. To be shifted. To let life, and more importantly God, move you where you need to go, not just where you want to stay. It means being open to change, even when it doesn’t make sense in the moment.

Because here’s the thing about that stone at the tomb. When it moved, it didn’t just change its own position. It revealed something greater. It opened the way for hope, for new life, for something no one expected.
That’s what moving stones do.

They don’t just change themselves. They create space for something bigger.

Maybe that looks like stepping into a new opportunity you’ve been avoiding. Maybe it’s having a conversation you’ve been putting off. Maybe it’s letting go of something that’s kept you stuck for far too long. Or maybe it’s simply choosing to show up differently for the people around you.

It doesn’t have to be dramatic.

But it does have to move.

Bricks build walls. Stones, when they move, can open doors.

So the question is simple.

Are you staying exactly where you’ve always been, holding your place, playing it safe?

Or are you willing to be moved, shaped, and used in a way that might just change not only your life, but someone else’s too?

Because one stays the same.

The other makes a difference.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Case of the Missing Roomba
excerpt: The Case of the Missing Roomba
content:
I never thought I’d say this out loud, but here we are…

My Roomba went missing.

Now, before you start picturing a dramatic search party, just know this wasn’t a casual “I can’t find it” situation. This was a full-blown investigation. Because at exactly 2:20 PM on Sunday, the camera showed her leaving her dock like she had a purpose. A mission. A destiny.

And then…

Nothing.

Gone. Disappeared. Vanished into thin air.

I checked the usual places. Under the couch. Behind the chairs. Along the walls like a good, obedient little vacuum should be. But no Roomba. No hum. No bumping into furniture like she’s got somewhere to be but no idea how to get there.

Just silence.

At one point, I started wondering if she had developed independence. Maybe she got tired of the routine. Maybe she thought, “You know what? I’ve cleaned enough of your mess. I’m out.”

I even checked the camera again like I was solving a crime. There she was… rolling out confidently… and then nothing. No return. No explanation. Just a robot with a dream and no GPS.

Eventually, after way too much time spent searching for something that literally cleans my floor, I found her.

Stuck. Wedged. Trapped in a place she had no business being.

And I laughed.

Because if we’re being honest… haven’t we all been there?

Not physically wedged under a piece of furniture (well… maybe not), but lost. Off course. Heading in a direction that seemed right at the time, only to find ourselves stuck, confused, and wondering how in the world we got there.

Life has a funny way of doing that.

We leave our “dock” with good intentions. We’ve got plans. Direction. Motivation. We’re moving. Progress feels real. And then somewhere along the way, we drift. We take a turn we didn’t expect. We end up in a place we didn’t plan.

And suddenly… we feel lost.

What struck me, though, wasn’t just that my Roomba got stuck.

It’s that I went looking for her.

I didn’t just say, “Well, guess she’s gone.” I searched. I checked. I paid attention. I followed the last place I saw her until I found where she ended up.

And that right there… that’ll preach a little.

Because in our own lives, when we feel lost, stuck, or completely off track, it’s easy to think we’ve gone too far. That maybe we’ve drifted too much. That no one’s coming to find us.
But the truth is… we’re not just wandering around unnoticed.

We’re sought after.

Even when we don’t realize it. Even when we’ve wedged ourselves into places we had no business being. Even when we’ve gone quiet and stopped moving forward.

There is a God who sees exactly where we left our “dock.” Who knows the path we took. Who doesn’t lose track of us, even when we feel like we’ve disappeared.
And just like that Roomba…

We’re not meant to stay stuck.

Sometimes we just need a little help getting unstuck. A little grace. A little redirection. Maybe even someone to pick us up, set us back where we belong, and say, *“Alright, let’s try this again.”*
And here’s the best part…

Being lost doesn’t mean you’re gone.
It just means you haven’t been found yet.

So if you’re feeling a little off course, a little stuck, or like you’ve wandered further than you intended…

Take heart.
You’re not forgotten.
You’re not overlooked.
And you’re definitely not beyond being found.

Even if it takes a little searching.
Even if it takes a little time.

Because sometimes, the things we lose…
are just waiting to be found again.

And thankfully, we’ve got a God who’s really good at finding things.
Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Power of Rain
excerpt: Rain makes a mess. It ruins plans, but it also teaches us.
content:
It’s a rainy Tuesday. The kind of day where the sky hangs low, the sidewalks shine, and everything feels just a little… slower. For many, it’s easy to look outside and call it what it appears to be on the surface, gloomy, messy, inconvenient. 

And trust me, I get it. I’ve got four corgis who treat a rain-soaked backyard like their personal mud spa, only to come barreling back inside with wet paws, questionable smells, and a mission to redecorate my floors with their “artwork.” It’s chaos. It’s real life. And it’s hard not to sigh a little.
But rain… rain is doing more than we give it credit for.

While we’re busy dodging puddles and wiping down floors for the fifth time, something bigger is happening. The earth is being refreshed. The grass is drinking it in. The trees, the flowers, the soil… all being nourished in ways we don’t always see in the moment. Rain has a purpose, even when it disrupts our plans or dampens our mood. It’s not here to ruin the day. It’s here to restore.

And maybe that’s where the perspective shift begins.

Because if we’re honest, our lives have rainy seasons too. Times when things feel heavy. When clarity is hard to find. When everything feels a little messy and out of order. We try to avoid those seasons. We pray them away. We ask, “Why now?” or “Why me?” But what if those moments are doing something deeper within us? What if they’re not here to break us down, but to build us up?

Rain softens hard ground so something new can grow. And in the same way, the challenges we face have a way of softening us… our hearts, our perspectives, our patience. They make room for growth, for reflection, for something new to take root in our lives. It may not feel good in the moment. Let’s be real, it rarely does.

 But it’s necessary.

Spiritually, rain has always carried meaning. It represents renewal, blessing, provision. It’s a reminder that even when the sky is gray, God is still at work. Quietly. Consistently. Faithfully. You may not see immediate results, but something is happening beneath the surface. Something is being prepared.
So maybe today isn’t just a rainy Tuesday.

Maybe it’s a reminder to slow down. To breathe a little deeper. To find peace in the rhythm of the rain hitting the roof. To laugh when your dogs track mud across the floor… again. To recognize that even in the mess, there’s meaning. Even in the inconvenience, there’s purpose.

And maybe, just maybe, the rain is exactly what we needed.

Not to ruin the day… but to refresh it.

Remember be kind to yourself and to others.

title: A box of Straws
excerpt: Ever looked through a single straw?  A box of them?  What do you see?
content:
It’s funny the things that can teach us something about life. A straw isn’t exactly where you’d expect to find wisdom, but stay with me on this one.

If you hold up a single straw and look through it, your view is narrow. You see one small slice of what’s in front of you. Everything else disappears. The edges close in, and suddenly the world feels smaller, more confined, more certain than it actually is. And if we’re honest, that’s how many of us move through life. We look through our own “single straw” made up of our experiences, opinions, hurts, and assumptions. We convince ourselves that what we see is the whole truth.

But it isn’t.

Now imagine looking through a box of straws. Not one. Not two. A whole bundle. It’s not as clean. It’s not perfectly focused. In fact, it can feel a little chaotic at first. But it’s wider. There’s depth. There are layers. You begin to notice things you would have completely missed with that single, narrow view.

That image is meant to be metaphorical. Life isn’t meant to be seen through one fixed lens. The “box of straws” represents perspective, diversity of thought, lived experiences, and the willingness to accept that truth is often bigger than what we can personally see. It reminds us that understanding doesn’t come from narrowing our view—it comes from expanding it.

And that’s really the heart behind this reflection.

This isn’t just about straws. It’s about how we see people. It’s about how quickly we judge, how firmly we hold onto our assumptions, and how often we resist stepping outside of what feels familiar. When we only look through one straw, we miss the fullness of someone’s story. We miss their struggles, their growth, their reasons. We reduce people to moments instead of seeing the journey.

Spiritually, this matters more than we often realize. Scripture reminds us that our perspective is limited. “For now we see only a reflection as in a mirror; then we shall see face to face.” (1 Corinthians 13:12). That means what we see right now is partial. It’s incomplete. God sees the full picture—the entire box, if you will—while we’re often stuck looking through a single opening.

That should humble us.

It should slow us down.

It should make us more careful with our words, our judgments, and our reactions.
Because when we accept that we don’t see everything, we create space for something powerful—understanding.

Looking through a “box of straws” is an intentional choice. It means choosing to listen before reacting. It means allowing yourself to sit in someone else’s perspective, even if it challenges your own. It means recognizing that two things can exist at once—your experience is valid, and so is theirs.
And in that space, growth happens.

We start breaking down the barriers we’ve built over time. The walls of pride. The walls of fear. The walls of “I’m right and that’s all there is to it.” Those barriers keep us isolated. They keep us disconnected. And more often than not, they keep us from loving others the way we’re called to.

Because love requires a wider view.

Jesus modeled this perfectly. He didn’t operate from a narrow lens. He saw beyond labels, beyond mistakes, beyond what others chose to focus on. He met people where they were, but He never left them there. He understood their story, and in doing so, He changed their story.

That kind of perspective changes everything.

When we begin to widen our view, we become more patient. More compassionate. More willing to extend grace. We stop reacting so quickly and start understanding more deeply. We realize that people aren’t problems to solve—they’re stories to learn from.

And maybe that’s the real purpose behind this simple image.
Not just to think differently—but to live differently.

To choose acceptance over assumption.
To choose understanding over judgment.
To choose connection over division.

Because the world doesn’t need more people looking through a single straw, convinced they see it all.
It needs people willing to pick up the whole box, even if it’s messy, even if it’s uncomfortable, and say, 
“There’s more here than I realized.”

And that’s where real growth begins—not when everything becomes clear, but when we become open.
So the next time you pick up a straw, take a second. Think about what you’re seeing—and what you might be missing.

Because life was never meant to be viewed through such a narrow space.
Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Power of One
excerpt: One Person holds a lot of power. Many times its not unleashed.
content:
The Power of One

There’s an old belief that only the loudest voices make a difference. That impact belongs to celebrities, influencers, politicians, or people standing on giant stages with spotlights pointed at them. Social media has only amplified that idea. We scroll through perfectly curated lives, follower counts, viral videos, and highlight reels, and somewhere along the way many people begin believing that if they are not “known,” then somehow they are not meaningful.

But the truth is much simpler and much more powerful.

One person can change everything.

Not because they have a million followers. Not because they trend online. Not because the world applauds them. But because they showed kindness to someone who desperately needed it. Because they listened. Because they encouraged. Because they stayed when others walked away. Because they chose compassion over criticism.

The power of one has never really been about fame. It has always been about presence.

Every single day we encounter people carrying burdens we know nothing about. The cashier who smiles while fighting exhaustion. The coworker silently battling anxiety. The teenager trying to figure out where they belong. The older gentleman sitting alone at breakfast hoping someone says good morning. 

The friend who keeps saying “I’m fine” because they don’t know how to explain they’re drowning.
And sometimes the very thing that changes their direction is not some grand gesture. Sometimes it is simply one person who notices them.

One conversation.
One hug.
One compliment.
One prayer.
One moment of patience.
One act of grace.

That’s the beautiful thing about humanity. We often underestimate the ripple effect of ordinary moments.
You may never fully know the impact you have on others. A kind word you spoke years ago may still live in someone’s heart today. 

A moment where you chose understanding instead of judgment may have restored somebody’s faith in people. Even your quiet consistency can become an anchor for someone struggling to hold themselves together.

Scripture reminds us of this repeatedly. Jesus did not always teach massive crowds. Some of His most powerful moments happened one-on-one. One woman at the well. One blind man. One grieving family. One lost soul at a time. The world often celebrates large numbers, but God has always understood the value of one.

And maybe that is something we need to remember more often.

We spend so much time comparing ourselves that we forget our assignment is not to become someone else. It is to use whatever gifts, opportunities, and heart we have been given right where we are. You do not need a platform to inspire people.

 You simply need a willingness to care.

Your daily interactions matter.

How you speak to people matters.

How you treat strangers matters.

How you react when frustrated matters.

How you encourage your children, coworkers, friends, and neighbors matters.

Those little moments become the threads that hold communities together.

Some of the greatest people you will ever meet may never appear on television or go viral online. They are the teachers who encourage struggling students. The dispatchers answering calls in the middle of the night. The nurses holding someone’s hand during fear. The parents working overtime to provide. The friend who always checks in. The person who quietly gives without needing recognition.

That is real influence.

Not measured in likes, views, or shares.

Measured in lives touched.

The older I get, the more I realize that impact is rarely loud. Most meaningful things in life happen quietly. A seed planted in good soil does not make noise while it grows. Yet eventually it becomes something strong enough to provide shade for others.

Maybe today someone needs your encouragement.
Maybe they need your forgiveness.
Maybe they simply need your smile.
Maybe they need to know somebody sees them.

Never underestimate what the power of one can do.

Because one conversation can restore hope.
One act of kindness can interrupt despair.
One person can help another believe they matter again.

And perhaps the greatest part of all is this: every single one of us already possesses that power.
Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Great Pollen Invasion
excerpt: The Pollen. The sneezes.  The nasty cars.  Yet, Pollen tells us something more... Growth
content:
Dr. Nick’s Matter of Perspective: The Great Pollen Invasion

Every spring, Mother Nature decides she is going to repaint the world in fifty shades of yellow-green dust. Cars? Covered. Patio furniture? Covered. Sidewalks? Covered. My black shorts after letting the corgis outside for five minutes? Absolutely ruined.

Pollen season is nature’s way of reminding us that nothing in life stays clean for long. Especially not with corgis involved.

I walked outside the other morning and looked at my vehicle. It appeared less like a car and more like a powdered donut abandoned in a parking lot. I wiped one finger across the hood and created what looked like an archaeological discovery site. Somewhere, I’m convinced pollen is sitting in the air laughing at all of us while we blindly keep buying allergy medicine and pretending we are winning the battle.

And then there are the sneezes. Good Lord, the sneezes. The kind that make you question your survival and briefly see your ancestors waving at you from the other side. You sneeze so hard you pull a muscle in your neck and suddenly need a recovery period. Meanwhile, the dog looks at you like you’ve personally offended the household.

The corgis do not help matters. They come inside looking like little fuzzy Swiffer dusters. Mogwai especially seems proud of himself, like he personally rolled in every pollen patch in St. Charles County just to bring spring indoors. Their paws leave little yellow footprints through the house, and somehow there is always one tuft of fur floating through the air like a tumbleweed in an old western movie.

But as aggravating as pollen can be, there is also something deeper hidden in all of it.

Pollen is messy because growth is happening. Trees are budding. Flowers are blooming. The earth is waking back up after a long winter. The irritation is actually evidence that life is returning.

That got me thinking about our own lives. Sometimes growth feels inconvenient. Healing can be messy. Change can irritate us. New seasons in life often arrive with discomfort before beauty fully appears. We want the flowers without the pollen. The breakthrough without the struggle. The joy without the cleanup.

But life rarely works that way.

Some of the greatest growth in our lives comes during seasons where things feel unsettled. Maybe you are in a season right now where you feel overwhelmed, emotionally exhausted, or frustrated because everything feels chaotic. Maybe you feel like life has coated your spirit in a thick layer of pollen and you cannot quite catch your breath.

Yet even there, something may still be blooming inside you.

Faith reminds us that God often works in seasons. Winter does not last forever. Dormant things can live again. Strength can return. Joy can return. Purpose can return. Even when life feels irritating and messy, growth may still be quietly happening underneath the surface.

The truth is, pollen eventually washes away. The rain comes. The air clears. But the growth it helped create remains.

So maybe the next time you wipe yellow dust off your windshield for the seventeenth time this week, take a moment before muttering words that would make your pastor nervous. Remember that sometimes the mess around us is proof that something beautiful is still growing.

Even if it is currently trying to attack your sinuses.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Feeling of being "Unpopular"
excerpt: Ever felt like you don't belong or matter?
content:
There’s a scene in Wicked where the song “Popular” plays, and while it’s meant to be humorous and lighthearted, I have always quietly connected more with the opposite side of it. The feeling of being unpopular. The feeling of being the one picked last. The outsider. The person who never quite felt like they belonged in the room, even while standing in the middle of it.

I remember elementary school gym class vividly. Two captains standing there picking teams while everyone shifted around nervously pretending not to care. Name after name would be called, and there I stood. Waiting. Smiling awkwardly. Trying to act like it didn’t bother me.

But it did.

Every single time.

Eventually, you begin preparing yourself mentally for rejection before it even happens. You start believing maybe there really is something wrong with you. Maybe you’re too awkward. Too different. Too loud. Too quiet. Too emotional. Too much. Or maybe… not enough.

For me, that feeling followed me from elementary school through high school. I was picked on, tormented, laughed at, and harassed. The painful part is I never really told anyone. I carried it quietly because that’s what many kids do. You learn to survive by pretending everything is fine.

And honestly? Some scars don’t disappear simply because you become an adult.

People often assume that if someone becomes successful, confident, funny, visible, or accomplished, then somehow the hurt disappears. But sometimes achievement is born because of pain. Sometimes the person trying hardest to make everyone laugh is also the person who spent years wondering why they weren’t enough.

So I worked.

I went to college.
Then more college.
Then even more college.

I got on television. I chased storms. I built platforms. I earned degrees. I accomplished goals people said I never would. Yet one of the hardest truths I have had to wrestle with is this: sometimes the people you hoped would be the proudest of you barely acknowledge the mountains you climbed to get there.

That hurts in a way that is difficult to explain.

Educational achievements mattered deeply to me because education became my escape, my purpose, and my proof that I could become something more than the labels others placed on me. Every late night studying, every paper written, every exam passed, every degree earned represented survival. It represented determination. It represented refusing to quit.

But there were moments where it felt like none of it truly mattered to the people whose validation I longed for most.

And when your accomplishments are minimized long enough, you begin wondering if you are being minimized too.

That pain sits quietly in people.

You smile through it.
You joke through it.
You keep achieving through it.

But deep down there is still a child asking, “Why am I never enough to be fully seen?”

I have spent a lot of my life feeling disconnected. Disengaged. Like I was somehow standing outside the circle of where I was supposed to belong. There have been moments where I genuinely questioned if my existence itself was somehow a mistake. Being born outside of marriage carried shame in certain spaces growing up, especially within religious expectations. Then add being gay into the equation, and there were moments where I felt like I was carrying a label before I even had a chance to understand myself.

Maybe people sensed I was different.
Maybe they targeted what they didn’t understand.
Maybe I wasn’t athletic enough.
Maybe I wasn’t “manly” enough.
Maybe I wasn’t interested in following the crowd.
Maybe I was simply an easy target because I was sensitive and different.

I don’t fully know.

What I do know is that words leave marks long after bruises fade.

And rejection has a way of teaching people to question their worth.

When my grandparents passed away, it felt like I lost the people who truly saw me. The people who made me feel safe. Supported. Wanted. Their love felt unconditional in a world where so much love often felt conditional. Losing them left a silence in my life that still echoes at times.

There are days where loneliness feels louder than the room around me.

Yet somehow… I still show up.

That’s the part I think people do not always see.

Despite the rejection, despite the bullying, despite feeling like an outsider much of my life, I still wake up trying to make others smile. I still try to be the light in the room. I still try to encourage people, support them, make them laugh, and remind them they matter.

Maybe that comes from understanding what it feels like when nobody reminds you.

Pain has a strange way of creating two kinds of people: those who become bitter and those who become softer toward others because they know what hurt feels like firsthand.

I never wanted someone else to feel invisible the way I often did.

So I overcompensate with kindness.
With humor.
With encouragement.
With presence.

Because if I can help someone else feel seen, then maybe some of my own pain gains purpose.

I’ve spent years reading Scripture, praying, wrestling with faith, and trying to understand where I fit. But the more I read the Bible, the more I notice something powerful: God consistently loved the people society rejected.

The outcasts.
The overlooked.
The wounded.
The doubted.
The people others pushed aside.

Jesus sat with those people.
Walked with those people.
Loved those people.

Not once did He say, “Fix yourself first, then come to Me.”

That matters.

Because somewhere along the line many people were taught that being different somehow means being broken. But I do not believe God creates human beings accidentally. I don’t believe compassion, empathy, or authenticity are mistakes.

And despite the battles in my own mind, I continue coming back to this truth:

God does not make mistakes.
And neither am I.

Some days that truth feels strong.
Other days it feels fragile.

But I hold onto it anyway.

Ironically, some of the purest love I’ve ever experienced comes from four short-legged corgis who could not care less about labels, popularity, status, or expectations. They don’t care about accomplishments or opinions. They don’t care who approves of me.

They just love me.

Fully.
Unconditionally.
Without hesitation.

And maybe humans could learn something from that.

I think there are many people walking around carrying the same feelings I carried growing up. The feeling of being unwanted. Unchosen. Unseen. Maybe they hide it behind humor. Success. Busyness. Sarcasm. Overachievement. Maybe they smile loudly while hurting quietly.

If that’s you, I want you to know something:

Being rejected by people does not mean you are rejected by God.

Being different does not make you less valuable.

And being unpopular does not mean your life lacks purpose.

Some of the most beautiful souls I know are people who spent years feeling like outsiders. Maybe because pain often creates empathy. Maybe because those who know loneliness become the people most willing to include others.

I still have moments where I feel like the outcast. The one who doesn’t fully fit. The one standing awkwardly in the background wondering where they belong.

But maybe life was never about fitting perfectly into the crowd.

Maybe it was about learning to stand authentically in who God created us to be.

And maybe the people who spent years feeling unpopular are actually the ones capable of loving others the deepest.

And maybe that is where purpose is born.

Not from popularity.
Not from being chosen first.
Not from fitting into everyone else’s expectations.

But from surviving what tried to break you and still choosing kindness anyway.

Maybe your story is not meant to make everyone comfortable. Maybe it is meant to help someone else sitting quietly in their own pain realize they are not alone. The kid being bullied. The adult hiding loneliness behind humor. The person wrestling with faith, identity, rejection, or self-worth. The one silently wondering if they matter.

They do.

And so do you.

I think some of the strongest people in this world are not the loudest or most celebrated. They are the people who wake up every day carrying invisible wounds and still find the strength to encourage others. The people who know heartbreak intimately but continue offering love anyway. The people who were rejected but refuse to become cruel.

That is strength.

That is courage.

That is resilience.

The truth is, our greatest pain often becomes the bridge that allows us to connect deeply with others. Empathy is born from understanding suffering firsthand. And perhaps those of us who spent years feeling unpopular are uniquely equipped to notice the people standing alone in the corner because we remember exactly what that felt like.

So if you feel disconnected, unseen, or forgotten today, please hear this:

Your life has value.
Your story matters.
Your existence is not accidental.

You do not have to earn the right to be loved.

And even if people failed to remind you of your worth growing up, that does not erase the truth of who you are today.

Keep showing up.
Keep loving people.
Keep making others laugh.
Keep being the light.

Because sometimes the people who struggled hardest to find light become the very people who shine it brightest for others.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

Dr. Nick
